counterpoints.text: The strongest objection to replace-style merge is upgrade drift: a new default list entry shipped later never reaches users who pinned that list. I accept this as the deliberate, forward-only cost, whereas list-merge's cost (un-removable defaults, a one-way breaking switch) violates 'no breaking changes after release'.

completed.currentPosition: Single hand-editable config file with namespaced per-command sections, plus a frozen replace-per-key merge contract and materialized defaults to close the silent list-drop gap.
completed.position: Single configuration file with namespaced [commands.<name>] sections remains correct; the contrarian's stress test in r1-msg-002 exposes a real defect in the merge layer, not in the layout, and it is fixable inside the single-file design by fixing the merge semantics rather than the file count.
completed.previousPosition: Single hand-editable config file with namespaced per-command sections, loaded by one parse-merge-with-defaults pass.

proposals.text: Freeze the merge contract at v1: defaults fill only omitted keys; any key the user writes wins verbatim, lists included (replace, never element-merge). Document as a stability guarantee.

On first run write a fully materialized config (or shipped commented defaults) so every list is visible and editable; 'add one entry' becomes 'edit the full list', eliminating silent-drop without list-merge logic.
Preserve unknown/future sections and keys on load but emit a non-fatal warning, so growth to ~12 commands stays additive and typos surface without breaking older files.

questions.text: Do we require new default list entries shipped later to reach users who customized that list? If yes, that pushes toward versioned-default migration on load rather than plain replace, and we must decide before v1 freezes the contract.

completed.reasoning: In r1-msg-001 I underspecified 'parse-merge-with-defaults' and treated additivity as a property of file layout. r1-msg-002 correctly shows the load-bearing risk lives in the per-key merge rule: with replace-style merge a user adding one list entry silently overrides the shipped default list, and switching to list-merge later would change every existing user's observed config (a forbidden breaking change). The fix is to make scalar-vs-collection merge behavior explicit and frozen at v1: user-written keys win verbatim (replace, lists included), and ship a materialized default config so lists are never silently half-specified. This keeps the loader a single deterministic parse-then-replace-per-key pass, keeps the file hand-editable with no hidden merge math, and stays additive from 6 to 12 commands.

references.comment: Extends my own declaration by specifying the merge contract I left underspecified.
Concedes the contrarian's merge-semantics point and resolves it within single-file.
